examples.title: Contiguous Allocation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to allocate contiguous blocks for a new file
examples.code:
ALGORITHM AllocateContiguous(fileSize)
BEGIN
  startBlock <- -1
  FOR each contiguous sequence of free blocks in disk DO
    IF length(sequence) >= fileSize THEN
      startBlock <- first block of sequence
      FOR i <- 0 TO fileSize-1 DO
        MarkBlock(startBlock + i, ALLOCATED)
        AddToFileAllocationTable(currentFile, startBlock + i)
      END FOR
      RETURN startBlock
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN -1  // No suitable space found
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm searches for a contiguous sequence of free blocks large enough to hold the file. It iterates through disk blocks looking for free sequences and allocates if found. If no contiguous space exists, it returns failure (-1), illustrating the external fragmentation problem.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is total disk blocks
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) besides the file allocation table

examples.title: Linked Allocation - Block Access
examples.description: Algorithm to access the nth block of a file in linked allocation
examples.code:
ALGORITHM AccessBlockLinked(fileHandle, blockNumber)
BEGIN
  // Start from first block of file
  currentBlock <- GetFirstBlock(fileHandle)
  
  // Traverse linked list to reach nth block
  FOR i <- 0 TO blockNumber-1 DO
    IF currentBlock == EOF THEN
      RETURN -1  // Block does not exist
    END IF
    currentBlock <- GetNextBlockPointer(currentBlock)
  END FOR
  
  // Read and return the data at current block
  RETURN ReadBlock(currentBlock)
END
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the sequential nature of linked allocation. To access any block, you must traverse all preceding blocks by following pointers. This shows why random access is O(n) in linked allocation.

examples.title: Indexed Allocation - Direct Access
examples.description: Algorithm to directly access the nth block using index block
examples.explanation: This shows the efficiency of indexed allocation for direct access. The nth block can be accessed in O(1) time by directly reading the pointer from the index block, without traversing any other data blocks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) to access nth block (constant time)

examples.title: Free Space Bit Vector Management
examples.description: Python implementation of bit vector for free space management
examples.explanation: This implementation demonstrates how bit vectors work for free space management. The bitmap allows quick checking of block status and can find contiguous blocks for contiguous allocation. The allocate_contiguous method shows how to handle external fragmentation detection.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for scanning, O(1) for single block allocation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of blocks
